GURREET AFFAIRS,

‘An Industrial Development Association has been
formed in Carlow. Mr. T. P. Gill announced that boots
to the value of one and a half millions sterling were im-
ported into Ireland last year | Now, the fact that several

~ boot factories exist in Ireland goes to show that the
industry is suitable to Ireland. In Carlow itself, the

enterprise of Mr. Michael Governey is responsiblo for a

boot factory. On the face of it there is plenty of room
for the expansion of the home trade in boots, and the

prospects before the contemplated new boot factory are
bright, so far as the extent of the home market is con-

" Mr, Gill also stated that £300,000 worth of

ag imported. No doubt the “truly Nationals’’

great deal of that—the LEADER is printed on

but after all what are they to do if the

yhad? There is only one firm, the North

of Ireland Paper Mills Co., Limited, making news paper
in Ireland so far as we know, and the note paper is
chiefly in the hands of the Saggart Dark Brothers, into
whose coffers we have turned tons of thousands of
pounds by our campaign, and who, in their turn, never
turned as much as a ha’penny into ours. The Chamber-
lain family, wo understand, are about to start paper-
making in Ireland.  Well, if Irish enterprise is not
equal to the task, it is well, even in these days of Sinn
Fein talk, to find base British Capitalists helping to
create industry in the country. What are the landlords
doing with the money they are getting? In the matter
of bacon and hams, Ireland—Ireland of the “Paddy
and his Pig’’ pictures—actually imported £1,540,000
worth. Most remarkable figure of all, no less than four
million pounds worth of fodder and feeding stuffs, ac-
cording to Mr. T. P. Gill, were imported by Irish agri-
culturalists last year. No wonder there is a shortage of
tillage and a continuance of emigration. The Irish
Industrial Revival has a wide field of operation before
it. As a commencement Carlow, now that it has started
an Association of its own, might wear its own boots.

A swindler named Tagg, from England, has been
found guilty of obtaining money under false pretences
from a few simple people in this country. This Tagg
advertised in the Irish Times as follows:— Manager
wanted for Dublin District; must invest £200; salary,
£150 per annum to commence and commission. Address
Z,”’ etc. We certainly would not insert such an ad-
vertisement in our paper without satisfying ourselves
that it was bona-fide. = One Mr. George Clampett, an
auctioneer, was caught in the net and parted with £50.
One associates auctioneers with a class of men-who, in
a special manner, “know their way about,’” and the fact
that one of the fraternity was fooled by this swindler
who advertised for victims in the Zrish 7imes, shows
how large is the fools’ margin ever present for the ex-
ploitation of swindlers. We suppose this Mr. George
Clampett, who was taken in before his cyes by this
clumsy British gaol-bird who advertised in the Irish
T'imes, would be too “shrewd’” now to risk twenty shil-
lings in the contemplated boot factory! Mr. James
Hehir, of Crusheen, Co. Clare, a farmer’s son, gave £5
on account of 100 shares that he agreed to take, and wes
appointed a manager at £5 a week, with commission.
Well, Mr. Hehir, from Clare, has at least got some
experience of the ways of the shady British world for
his £5, for needless to say he never got any salary. M.
Hehir was drawn into the swindle by sceing the ad-
vertisement in the Irish T'smes. Mr. Thomas Kane like-
wiso answered this advertisement in the Irish Times,
and got into communication with the British gaol-bird.
Kane was appointed  General Inspector for Ireland
at £4 a week. Io gave Tagg £5, but ho appears to
have been more shrewd than the others, for he subse-
quently stopped the cheque.

Why does the Zrish T'imes take such advertisements?
Porhaps if Mr. George Clampett, the auctioneer, ap-
plied to Mr. Algernon Locker, the latter, on behalf of
tho Trish Times Co., might refund him the £50, out of
which he was swindled by the gaol-bird who advertised
in the paper.

Now and again people write to us about alleged fraudu-
lent advertisements that appear in Irish papers. THow-
ever, we have something else to do besides pursuing in-
quirics concerning the alleged swindling advertisements
in other papers. Those who write us should, at least, at
first communicate with the papers that take the respon-

- sibility of inserting the advertisements for money down.
In view of the ?a.cc that our precious Dublin daily
papers continue to insert, for payment, advertisements
of filthy plays, we cannot be surprised if swindlers
Sometimes get space for their announcements.

“Motto for office use: If drinking whiskey inter-
feres with your business, give up your business.”” This
advice is taken from a “ humorous’’ post-card marked
“Printed in Great Britain.”’ No doubt, many in
Britain would like some Irish manufacturers who are
hitting them hard these times to take to whiskey, and
give up competing successfully in the markets with
them. A reader of the LEAbpER, who has been visiting
Killarney, writes us:— This is tho style of card that
is shown in some of the respectable-looking shops in
Killarney.”” Our correspondent also says:—“ When T
remonstrated I was told it sold better than any other.”!
Perhaps it suits Killarney, for however it may be with
whiskey-drinking, the town appears to be in a perma-
nent state of agreement to give up its business in so far
as keeping itself clean and tidy and looking after its
lanes is concerned.

The Secretary of the Executive Committeo of the
Gaelic League of the State of New York sends us a copy
of a strong resolution passed by that body condemning
those members of the Oughterard Board of Guardians
who voted against Dr, O’Beirn at the recent election
of medical officer.

In another column a contributor deals with the recent
article by Rev. Mr. Leslie, of Castlebellingham, which
appeared in the so-called Church of Ireland Gazette.
We have received the following from Sir Henry
Bellingham : —

“Sir—In a paper called The Church of Ireland
Gazette a list recently appeared of some seventeen pre-
Reformation priests of Louth, during the years 1540-
1558, still in possession of their livings, which was given
as proof that the clergy of this county accepted the
Reformation. A letter was thereupon addressed in
reply, but as it was (for reasons best known to the
editor) not inserted, I send the substance to you.

““ Sir—The contention might hold good if the follow-
ing postulates could be satisfactorily proved.

““(1) That the Protestant Reformation was fully
accomplished under Henry VIII.

“(2) That Queen Mary (in whose reign some of the
priests in this list died in possession of their livings)
insisted on livings being filled by the Reformed clergy.

“ (3) That Hugh Goodacre was Primate in 1556, 2.c.,
in Mary’s reign and three years after his own decease
(Mary had recalled Dowdall from the continent in 1553
and reinstated him in 1554).

“(4) That- Primate Dowdall, who was notoriously
opposed to the Reformation, appointed . priests to
parishes who accepted it.

““But in any case it is a well-known fact in history
that many in England and Ireland who rejected the
principles of the Reformation, held on, under the
various changes that took place between 1500 and 1600,
i.e., during the successive reigns of Tenry VIII.,
Edward, Mary, and Elizabeth, always anticipating a
return to the original state of things.—Yours truly,

‘“ TIENRY BELLINGHAM.

“ Castle Bellingham.”’

For dense and hopeless ignorance on all historical
matters, for wilful blindness to patent facts, for absurd
theories held with smug satisfaction, commend to us tho
ordinary Irish Protestant. The letter from Sir Henry
Bellingham, rejected by the so-called Church of Ircland.
Gazette, which we publish above, is one instance out of
many of how they wilfully shut their eyes to the light.
One of them will trot out some silly statements, and
when a calm refutation is made of them by a Catholic
they refuse to read it. We have seen the article of
Rev. Mr. Leslie, M.A., and when we noted the dates
on which his seventeen ‘‘ conforming ’’ priests died, we

- wondered whether the M.A. of Trinity had ever studied

Joyce’s ““ Child’s History of Ireland.”” Joyce tells ug
that Henry VILI. made little or no change in religion,
and opposed the progress of the “ Reformation!”’ " Fe

only to be found among the o(I'xcm.ls of the Government,
that many restored everything as it was, and that, even
in Elizabeth’s time, most of the pavishes were held by

Trinity is as hopeless about history as it has already

What is to become of the poor Bungs in the County
of Cavan? At the Total Abstinence Ite-Union for part
of the county—West Cavan—which was held in  the
grounds of St. Patrick’s College, Cullies, there were,
according to our contemporary I'he A nglo-Celt, no less -
than fourteen thousand persons present! Bung down
there had better lose no time in shifting into
the boot manufacturing or other such produc-
tive industry. Two years ago the  re-union
was attended by the people of both divisions of
the county, but this year we learn that ‘‘ for the sake
of convenience, and because of the growing number of
teetotalers,’’ there were two demonstrations. 'The first
demonstration, held at Bailicborough, brought together
over five thousand persons; the last one, as we have
scen, is said to have gathered fourteen thousand. Well,
Bung’s despair is Ireland’s hope.

“The Tower Press Booklets ’’ comprise so far small
volumes of ‘‘long felt wants,”” published by Maunsell
and Co., Ltd., of Dublin. Though no doubt priceless
—for ‘‘ Cackleton ”’ and the “ Sober Nationalist ”’ of
Lincoln Place have had each, or are to have, a booklet
to themselves—they are offered to the public at a
shilling a piece ; it will be remembered that Dana, the
mighty one, was only sixpence. One of these shilling
“long felt wants’’ contains another * priceless trea-
sure,”’ a lecture delivered by the unconsciously comic
old gentleman who is spending the grey twilight of his
life in Ely Place. 1t might have been called Old
Moore’s Almanack, and we cannot tell why it is styled
‘“ Reminiscences of the Impressionist Painters.” We
have not reviewed any of the “Tower Press Booklets'’ so
far, and that is scarcely courteous to a publishing firm.
Mr. Moore, in his anecdotage comprises forty-
cight small pages, which, at a shilling, works
cut exactly at one farthing a page. It is cer-
tainly priceless, for there are some works whose value
to the public could not be expressed in money, even 1f
all the moncy of the world were gathered together;
there are others that are priceless because the coins of
this realm do not go below a farthing. Mr. Moore, in
his (ancc)dotage, is undoubtedly priceless, and for
which particular reason the intelligent reader can decide
for himself.

Mr. Bdoore says:—“All conventions, of politics,
sociely, and creed, yes, and of art, too, must be cast
into the melting pot; he who would be an artist must
melt down all things; he must discover new formulas,
new moulds, all the old values must be swept aside, and
he must arrive at a new estimate. The artist should
keep himself free from all creed, from all dogma, from
all opinion.”” This is pretty from an old man who was
only recently *“ converted *’ to the Thirty-nine Articles.
It is plain  from this view of this ‘verb
to the Church of Ireland’’ that he is not an
arbis—on his own showing he could not be
ons, as he has not kept himself free from
dogma! Again, in this booklet we read— “He
the (artist) is almost unmaware of your moral
codes, he laughs at them when he thinks of them, which
is rarely, and he is unashamed as alittle child.”” Well,
it would be hard to picture the blush of shame on the
tender cheoks of the recent *“’vert.”” According to the
moral code one should tell the truth, but the artist, we

are told, laughs at moral codes when he thinks of
% ?lmm‘_; 16 is reassuring to know that George, being a
vert to the ‘Church of Ireland”’ and therefore a
believer in dogmas, cannot be an artist! In some rail-
Wway termini the timid traveller veads with awe, “Be-
ware of pickpockets’’; would it not be well if the
County Councils of Ireland, in view of the attitude of

artists towards the moral code, put up warning
notices, ‘“ Bewave of Artists.’”
In tthe peroration of this wvolume we aro

told: “Life is a rose that withers in the iron fist
of dogma, and it was France that forced open the
deadly fingers of the ecclesiastic and allowed the rose to
blopm again.””  Captain Wade-Thompson, the Pervert
Priests’ Protector, had better make a call on this
curious 'vert; this weighty asset of the Church of Eng-
land’s Faithful Garrison in Ireland!

British Hop Bitters must be suffering from a bad
ieakage in Ireland. Many of our ‘‘ patriotic’’ papers,
as our readers know, are content to be the medium of
advertising this British drink, notwithstanding that
many firms in Ireland make excellent hop bitters. We
have before us a show card from the British Hop
Bitter firm. Itisa ‘“ green’’ show card ‘ designed and
printed in Ireland.”” Tt represents an Irish King clasp-
ing the shoulder of an ancient Irish soldier with his left
hand and grasping a glass of British Hop Bitters in his
right hand. We take it the 4no-Rig is supposed to be
saying ‘“Stdince.”  There is a melancholy wolf-dog
by his side, a sunburst and a round tower in the dis-
tance!  The design would do credit to Dermot
MacMurrough, ‘¢ who brought the Norman o’er.”” The
Irish address only of the British firm, that is, the
address of the office in Dublin through which the busi-
ness of dumping the liquid British dump is done, is
given. There is no mention of Sheffield.

A visitor to the Munster-Connacht Exhibition rells
us that another visitor, after going over the grounds
there, summed up his impressions of the display of 1\'}511
products by remarking to him that it was a revelation
in the way of Irish and particularly local industry, an
expression in which our informant thoroughly concurred.
We have heard that a lack of amusements has been
complained of, but to our mind the fact that Irish
manufacture and not a variety of somewhat irrelevant
side shows is the striking characteristic of the Exhibi-
tion is more a matter for congratulation than other-
wise. No doubt a big proportion of people who flock
to exhibitions go to amuse themselves, but after all the
real object of such exhibitions as the Limerick one is
not to provide light amusement for the crowd, bub to
show what is and may be manufactured in this country,
and we are glad to learn that, judged by this sensible
standard, the Munster-Connacht Exhibition is a great
success. The forthcoming Humphrey’s Dump in ‘the
Pembroke Township will probably collect mora to glad-
den the hearts of the trippers in the matter of imported
Cheap Jack attractions, but the Tndustrial Revival has
more to gain by exhibitions like the Munster-Connacht

one than by hundreds of International impositions.

Many of our readers who have not heen to the Exhi-
bition will perhaps be surprised to learn that pianos are

We are glad to learn of the splendid progress of the
Irish College recently erected at Ring, owing mainly
to the efforts of the Rev. Dr. Sheehan, Maynooth, author
of Sean Caint na nOéipe. There are at present about
thirty students at the College, some of them having
come from Galway and Roscommon, while one hails all
the way from Chester, England. The professors are
the Rev. Dr. Henebry (Irish Composition, Old and
Middle Irish and Irish Music), Rev. Dr. Sheehan
(Phonetics and Grammar), Mr. P. O’Kiely, Gaelic
League Organiser (Reading and Conversation), and Mr.
D. Stokes (Method of Teaching and Object Lessons).
Ring is probably the most Irish speaking parish in
Munster.  Here Irish is the home language of the
entire native population, of the children at play, the
farmer in the field, the fisherman on the deep. No
better place could have been selected for the erection
of a college for the studying of Irish. In the evening
the young men and women of the parish attend at the
school in large numbers and are taught to read and
write Irish. Some of them have progressed so well as
to be able to read the works of Tadhg Gaedhealach
and Paddy Denn, and the Irish articlesin Dr. Sheehan’s
Sean Caint na nOéire. The erection and equipment of
the College has entailed considerable expense, and sym-
pathisers with this effort to train teachers of the Irish
language and to keep it alive in one of the very few
districts in which it lives, as the everyday language of
the people can render the material assistance by direct-
ing money to defray expenses and to purchase books
to form the nucleus of a College Library. All subscrip-
tions will be thankfully received by the Rev. Dr.
Henebry or the Rev. Dr. Sheechan at the College.
The fee for the session is £1, but for those who cannot
remain during the full month special terms will be
arranged. Good accommodation can be had in Ring
within ten minutes’ walk of the College, at a ‘very
moderate charge. The present session, which opened
the 1st inst., will continue until the first week in
September. All information regarding fees, lectures,
ete., will be given by the secretaries, Messts, P. O’Kiely
and J. O’Flynn.

Ex-Sergeant Dooney was a policeman in Castle-
connell. He has now retired from the field of his life
““labour ” no doubt on a substantial pension. Need-
less to say, in patriotic and generous Castleconnell the
retirement of such a magnate into private life could not
be allowed to pass without the inevitable testimonial. At
a meeting in the local Coucert Hall, on July 26th, it
was decided to present the retired premier of Castle-
connell with a ‘“suitable testimonial.”” There was a
final meeting on August 5th. The chairman of the
committee is a B.A. of the University where the
notorious spellers come from ; he is likewise, we believe,
a Mr. Bung. The Hon. Sec., Mr. M. Murnane, is also
a Mr. B. Of the twelve ordinary members of the com-
mittee, five are Bungs. As possibly, though not pro-
bably, there may not be enough bungeries in Castle-
connell to adequately cater for the thirst of the inhabi-
tants, might we suggest that ¢ the suitable testimonial”’
to the retired policeman should be a new bungery !

The parish of Ring, in the county of Waterford,
offers an interesting study in Irish abnormality. There
is an Irish summer school there with which two dis-
tinguished clergymen are identified, the Rev. Dr.
Henebry and Rev. Dr. Sheehan, of Maynooth. Every
child in the parish, old enough to speak any language,
knows Irish, yet Irish is not taught in either the boys’
school or the girls’ school ; neither of the teachers know
Irish. The Catechism is taught in English only, and
we hear that it might as well be taught in Greek as far
as the children are concerned. Stranger still, though
the parish priest and the curate are Trish speakers, they
never preach in Irish to the people, though about
ninety per cent. of the grown people speak little or no
English. Over the way, at the Irish summer school,
closely identified with such distinguished Catholic eccle-
siastics as Rev. Dr. Henebry and Rev. Dr. Sheehan, of
Maynooth, the latter the author of Sean Caintna n oéipe
the people flock to hear and learn the Irish that is
boycotted in the school. Truly, Ring is a particularly
abnormal place even in this abnormal country.

Tho first night of the OQireachlas was a groat success.
Tho Round Room was crowded by an attentive audience.
Mr. Padraig MacSweeney, of Fermoy, delivered the .
Oireachtas oration in effective style, and the ode by Mr.
C. Desmond was rendered by him with the Ballyvourney
blas. All the singers did very well. Needless to say,
the songs by Mr. Seamus Clandillon and his inimitable
way of singing them did not tend to send the audience
asleep. Iis rendering of the humorous skif, Tpucait
& Baba, that originally appeared in the LEADER
of February, was very taking.

Tue WARrReNPoINT ‘‘ FEIS.” A

Str,—In the reference ou made in the last issue ?f
your paper to the entertainment a local committee in .
Warrenpoint is organising for the 15th inst., a question
is raised as to what constitutes a branch of the Gaelic
League which deserves the consideration of the Coipoe
Snota. If a branch of the Leaguo exists in the place
referred to, I would like to know when it was consti-
tuted, how many meetings—committee or general—
have been held during the past year, and the nature of
tho work, if any, accomplished.” If my information is
correct, and I have no reason to doubt it, the local
branch, such as it was, lapsed a good while ago. The
offorts of the Coipue Ceanncaip to rehabilitate it
proved futile, and that body had to transfer its opera-
tions to a district where more congenial soil exists.

A few years ago the town was represented on the
district committee, as well as Rostrevor and Killowen.
The male National teacher in Rostrevor has continued
his connection with that body, but the representatives
of the other places have not taken any part in its work
during the past two or three years.

They refused to even assist at the last district peip,
which was held in Omeath about the end of June, and
I do not believe that 20 people from Warrenpoint
attended the gathering, although they now show great
anxiety to forward the movement by getting up a show
which will include, I understand, a “ sack race,’’ in addi-
tion to a “ wheel-barrow ”’ and “ donkey race.” What
place these things hold in an Irish literary festival I do
not know, but I have no doubt the enthusiasts (?) of
Warrenpoint will be able to tell.

When a collection was made in Warrenpoint to meet
the initial expenses of the Omeath peip there was only
a few shillings obtained, and I understand the snubs
and sarcasm which accompanied the donations made the
collectors sorry they solicited any. Who are the guaran-
tors of the coming event? and how much has each made

THE OIREACHTAS AND INDUSTRY,

NDUSTRY has the Pillar Room, Rotunda, to itself

this ycar, and those who do not [ike traces of
“ machinery and commerce ’’ need not go near this rcom
in their quest after the “ beautiful.””” The Indusfrial
Show is an excellent little National Exhibition on a
small scale. It is well laid out, and on Monday, though
there werc a good number of visitors, it was easy to
movo about from stall to stall. It is well to have the
language movement closely allied with the revival of
“machinery and commerce.”” These may be  vulgar”
things, no doubt, but as it so happens they are necessary
to nation-making, which is the objective of the Irish-
Ireland movement. If the vulgarity of machinery
and commerce ’’ is necessary to modern progress, let us
have as much of it as possible by ‘all means for the sake
of Ireland; mute, inglorious Donatellas are unsatisfac-
tory stuft out of which to make a nation.

In the woollen department we missed the Dripsey and
Foxford Stalls, but if Foxford and Dripsey are not
there we are sure they are well represented on the backs
of the visitors. There is a display of woollen goods
from Avoca, and we do not remember to have heard of
the existence of that mill before. The stone industry
is represented by specimens from Donegal and Clare.
Messrs. Watson, who work these quarries, display spe-
cimens of stone from both, and there is a neat mantel-
piece that builders and those who deal in builders’
materials would do well to inspect. The Dublin Work-
ingmen’s Industrial Association display a serviceable
collection of Irish made furniture. This furniture in-
dustry is one of the many big fields lying fallow for
lack of the enterprising spirit amongst those who are in
a position to work it. Irish furniture of a kind that
would fall within the purchasing power of the villa
resident and the artisan is badly needed; it is made
here and there, but the supply is not sufficient.

The Irish publishing firms are in great force. Browne
and Nolan’s; Sealy, Bryers; The Catholic Truth So-
ciety ; The Gaelic League; Fallons, the well-known
school publishers; Duffy’s and Gills, all make interest-
ing displays. So also does one of the new publishing
firms: Messrs. Maunsell and Co.

There are boots from Hearne and Cahill’s, of Water-
ford, and from Governey’s, of Carlow; and of course
there are boot and other polishes. An Irish Industrial
Luxhibition would look lonely without a display from
Malone Brothers, and another from Messrs. McMena-
min. In the polishing and cleaning line there is a
brand new Irish industry—the “Ollo’’ pads. Our
readers will remember the brief outlines of the history
of the starting of this industry which appeared in a
recent issue of the Leaper. The pads, made out of
Ireland, took the Irish market by storm, and the pro-
prictor, who has factories in Great Britain, quickly
determined that as ho was selling the pads in Iveland,
ho would make them in Ireland also.

Soap—the very name is enough to give the cult of the
“beautiful ” a shock—is well in evidence—common
soap, toilet soap, shaving sticks, and so forth. Both
Barrington’s and Dixon’s demonstrate what a catching
display can be made out of soap alone. After all,
though highly-priced commodities untouched by
machinery are interesting, yet if only half a dozen shops
that now sell ful'oign and boycott Irish soap were in-
duced to push instead of boycott the latter, the change
would mean more as a solution of the emigration pro-
blem than the sale of a few hand-made articles about
which a score of newspaper articles and puffs had been
written. ~ 'We want more Irish soap in Ireland, and

even the brains of some of the cult of benu@y”
would not be the worse of a good scrubbing by Barrm»g,:—
ton’s Double Orown, Dixon’s Favourite, or Crean’s
Gladiator.

Messrs. Butler, the musical instrument firm, amongst
other things show a set of pipes mado to the order of a
Catholic clergyman. Mr. O’Neill exhibits a fow speci-
mens of his Dublinamade Lucania bicycles. 4
Despond shows a specimen of his workmmlship in ﬁbr?us
plaster. Mr. Despond, a Frenchman, as his name im-
plics, has been five years in Ireland, and now employs
eight Irishmen at this industry.

The show case of Messrs. Hopkins and Iopkins has a
special interest for all such as G.A.A secretaries and
members of Feis Committees who deal in badges and
medals.  Messrs. Hopkins have recently paid special
attention to this extensive branch of Irish industry, and
during this season they made to order about fifteen hun-
dred medals and badges.

Taking it all round, the Industrial Exhibition is well
worth a visit, and we hope that everyone who visits the
Oireachtas will not leave without making a tour of
inspection in the Pillar Room.

But when it is seen, fresh, wholesome and clean,

We'll still have our vile-smelling plays, my boys,
Our Saphos and our Kitty Greys, my boys.
Spring Chickens and stuff offensive enough,

Not nto the Liffey they’re thrown, my boys,

With puff decorations full blown, my boys.

No talk of a Main Drainage there, my boys,
The lucre of-filth they can’t spare, my boys.

Oh, no; let the Liffey be clean, my boys,

Another Main Drainage we nced, my hoys,

(o University blacklegs are on the alert, Foyr

some time past they have, it seems, been. con.
sumed with zeal for the settlement of the University
question. Zeal for such a causo is something quite new
in that quarter. Throughout the agitation, such as i
has been, for justice to Catholics in the domain of higher
education they have not been particularly prominent;
and if they have been at all active, their activity re-
flects more credit on their humility than on their public
spirit. ’

Not very long ago it was urged in these columns that,
in the future stages of the struggle made by Catholics
for educational justice, they should be politely told to
stand aside. This advice was tendered in the interests of
Catholicity and Nationality alike. The writer feared
that if they were allowed to interfere, they would, af
the opportune moment, make a supreme effort to cap-
ture such University or College as might be offered to
Catholics, and to constitute themselves the dominant
element in its government and administration. This
would have been bad enough, but what has actually
taken place is even worse. Having done little or noth-
ing to force or expedite a settlement which would be
equitable from the Catholic standpoint, they now lend
themselves to an intrigue hatched in Trinity College,
whose sole aim is to defeat any settlement whatever
that would be in the smallest degree acceptable to the
vast majority of the Catholics of Ireland. Tt is just like
them. They are ncver content except when they are
giving away or compromising the Catholic cause, as far
as it lies in their power to do so. How Irish Catholics
have ever been foolish enough to trust them with even
the smallest share in the University struggle passes
comprehension. They are in our day typical represen-
tatives of those Catholics of the Pale who, at a critical
juncture in our history, made themselves the tools of
Ormonde in his intrigues for the destruction of the Con-
federate cause.

Their assurance is amazing. Are there no Catholics
in Ireland except the *Cawtholics?”’  Are there no
others to be considered in this matter except a small
moiety of Catholics. who “reside in or near Dublin;’’
excepl those who have been educated in Trinity, or
who have sont their sons to Trinity or to English
schools and Universifies; except those who are Torics,
or Whigs, or place-men or place-hunters; except those
who have nothing in common with the vast majority of
the Irish people except the profession—in some cases
the not very robust profession,—of the same religion?
It is characteristic of them that they should lie low
during the fight; that they should, by their incon-
sistency and tolerance-proving and lack of back-bone,
impede and retard the success of the struggle; and that
at the eleventh hour, they should interpose to do their
utmost to give the settlement a direction which would
suit their own snobbishness, servility, West-Britonism

PROTESTANTS.

HE Rev. Mr. Leslie, of Castlebellingham, who
made himself notorious by his denunciation of

tho Sunday Feis as a desecration of the Lord’s Day, has
como out now in the réle of an historian. Ie has
lately been studying the Armagh Registers and other
records, and has made an extraordinary discovery
which he publishes in the pages of the so-called Church
of Ircland Glazette. The discovery that this budding his-
torian has made is no less than that the Catholic clergy
of Louth went over body and soul to the Protestant
Reformation. THow did he make this startling dis-
covery? Ie found from the reg'xisbers that the clergy,

" flesh.

_ and laity alike.

- seventeen in all, continued as rectors of their parishes

““after the Reformation had been accomplished,” and
thereupon concludes that they “ must have accepted the
Reformation.” Taking up the list he gives, I find that
several of these died in the reign of Henry VIIL., one
as far back as 1540, and four of them died in tho reign
of Queen Mary in possession of their parishes. Only
two out of the seventeen priests brought forward by
Mr. Leslie as having. become Protestants were living
when Queen Elizabeth made the first serious attempt
to introduce Protestantism into Ireland. An ordinary
knowledge of the saliont facts of the history of our
own country might be expected from an M.A. of
Trinity College, Dublin, before he presumed to write
about it. But some people are so dense that they are
not aware of the depth of their own ignorance,  What
a ridiculous figure Mr. Leslic cuts in blazoning forth
his discovery to his fellow-clergymen. Are they and ho
aware that King Henry VIII., up to the ond of his
life, was burning people for denying the Mass and
‘Transubstantiation; in other words, for accepting the
principles of the Reformation? Are they and he aware
that Quesn Mary was a Catholic, and stopped the abor-
tive attempts at changes in religion attempted in Ire-
land in the reign of Edward VI.? Who but one utterly
ignorant of history could dare to affirm that the priests
who died in possession of parishes during his reign must
have gone over to the Reformation?  Mr. Leslie is,
indeed, utterly at sea in the period he writes about.
Fe speaks of a priest living under Primate Hugh
Goodacre in 1556, evidently not aware that this Hugh
Goodacre, who was intruded into the see in 1553 by
Edward VI., died three months after his consecration.
What is the good of antiquities without history? What
can a man learn by making extracts from old records
if he is so ignorant of historical facts that he is led to
draw most absurd conclusions from his lists? Irish
Catholic priests do not write much, but when they do
sit down to pen and paper, they generally know what
vhey are talking about. 1Irish Protestants, on the other
hand, profess great knowledge, and join antiquar
societies in such large numbers that they would almost
seem to enjoy a monopoly in these matters. But
when they come to the test of writing, they show an
utter absence of the power of historical criticism. They
have the dry bones, but they cannot cover them wilh
And the reason is that they cannot bear tho
truth. Ireland was Catholic—purely and wholly Catho-
lic—in days gone by, when all the old churches and
abbeys were built. The Faith then, as now, centred
round the Holy Sacrifice of the Mass. The Protestant
Reformation was rejected by the Irish people, clergy
It has been sustained ever sinco by
robbery and confiscation, and has always been tho
Church of the English Garrison in this country. These
are the plain facts of history. TFor somo time past,
Protestant writers have been stultifying themselves by
affirming that the Reformation brought no essential
change, that they are the representatives of the old

College. And so anxious was he to procure such mas-
ters that he gave the money, although he was mot sure
that a, Tlebrew Lecturer could be found for Trimity
College. TFor when giving the money he directed that

AN Trise DAY 1IN THE SCHOOLS. ;

Dear Sie—The annual meeting for the discussion and
arrangoemont of all projects having to do with the Lan-
guago Movement in Ireland being near at hand, T wish
to say a word on a matter which some time ago was
broached at a general meeting of a city branch of the
Gaolic League—viz,, making cach Saturday an TIrish
day in the schools throughout the country.

The meeting referred to did not take kindly to the
idea, mainly for the reason that it would involve too
great an addition to the labours of both pupils and
teachers, but this objection could, T think, be got over
more ways than one. In the first place the Saturday
school for Irish only should be left wholly voluntary
for both parties and all concerned. There is now suffi-
cient onthusiasm for the old language in this country
to ensure a satisfactory measure of success on a volun-
tary basis.

The work of this Trish day in the schools need not by
any means be confined to the language alone. It might
occasionally take in such matters as dancing, singing,
story-telling, history; anything calculated to foster and
develop the Irish spirit, which is, or would be if we had
it, such a potent force for the development of the
country.

As at present employed, the Saturday appears to hang
rather heavily on the children’s hands. In many cases
the most they seem able to do is to make themselves a
trouble to their parents.
